title: Pig nutrition and feed management
subtitle: What pigs need to eat at each stage of life, and how to manage feed to avoid waste and obesity.
excerpt: Pigs are not difficult to feed, but they are easy to overfeed. An obese pig has poorer reproductive performance, more difficult farrowings, and shorter productive life. Understanding the stages of a pig's nutritional needs prevents most feeding mistakes.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, pig nutrition guide. NADIS, pig feeding factsheet. Compassion in World Farming, welfare-friendly pig husbandry notes. Agricultural and Horticultural Development Board, pig production manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A feeding system in which feed is available at all times in a hopper or feeder. Suitable for growing pigs being finished, where fast growth to slaughter is the aim. Not recommended for breeding sows or boars, which will over-consume and become obese.

A physical assessment of fat depth over the pig's back at a standardised point behind the shoulder. Used in breeding pigs to ensure sows are not too fat at farrowing, and to confirm they are not too thin at weaning. Very fat sows farrow with more difficulty and produce fewer viable piglets.

content.text: A pig's nutritional requirements change at every stage of its life: creep, weaner, grower, finisher, dry sow, in-pig sow, lactating sow. Commercial pig feeds are formulated for each stage. On a smallholding, knowing which stage your pig is at tells you which feed to buy.
Pigs being grown for meat can be fed on an

system using a grower or finisher pellet appropriate to their weight range. The transition from weaner pellet to grower pellet happens at around 30 to 40 kg liveweight; from grower to finisher at around 60 to 70 kg. As pigs approach slaughter weight, excess fat deposition increases. If you want a leaner carcass, reduce the feed allowance in the final 4 to 6 weeks.
A dry (non-pregnant) sow on ad lib feeding will become obese. Breeding pigs are fed to

target: you want a sow in good, lean condition at farrowing, not thin and not overfat. Feed allowances increase as pregnancy progresses, and increase again during lactation when the sow is producing milk for a large litter. A sow losing condition rapidly through lactation needs more feed, not less.

Pigs are omnivores and natural rooters. On a smallholding, rooting in a paddock contributes to their diet and provides enrichment. Pigs on pasture will supplement their pelleted ration with grass, roots, and invertebrates. This is beneficial, but do not reduce the concentrate ration based on apparent grazing; pigs rarely extract enough nutrition from pasture alone to maintain weight without concentrate feed.

It is illegal in the UK to feed pigs any food that has been in contact with a kitchen, including all household food waste, catering waste, and anything handled in a kitchen. This restriction applies regardless of whether the material has been cooked. The legal feed for pigs is commercially produced compound feed, homegrown grain or root vegetables that have not passed through a kitchen, and purpose-grown crops. Breach of this rule is a serious animal health offence.
